content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 27, 2012

Christy Quinlan
503 Dunbarton Circle
Sacramento, CA 95825

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-12-077

Dear Ms. Quinlan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment laws that may apply including but not limited to Public Contract Code Sections 10410 and 10411, Government Code Section 1090, or laws regarding incompatible activities such as Government Code Section 19990.

QUESTION

As the former Acting Secretary of the California Technology Agency (the "CTA"), what restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on:

(1) your future employment in the private sector as an IT procurement agent, who may approach government agencies for future sales, or

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 2

(2) your ability to work for a different agency as a retired annuitant?

CONCLUSION

(1) As the former Acting Secretary of the CTA, the one-year ban prohibits you from making an appearance or communication before the CTA, or any state agency whose budget, personnel, or operations are under the CTA's direction or control, in representation of another person, and for compensation, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property" such as an appearance or communication related to IT procurement, for one year after you leave the agency.

Additionally, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban that also applies to former state employees. Under this ban, you are permanently prohibited from ever participating in a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding involving the State of California, or assisting others in the proceeding, for compensation if you previously participated in the proceeding as a state officer or employee.

(2) Also note that should you return to the state as a retired annuitant for a different agency, the one-year and permanent bans may potentially apply if in your private capacity and for compensation you were to make an appearance or communication or assist another person in an appearance or communication before your former agency. Upon leaving the subsequent retired annuitant position, the one-year ban would begin anew for appearances before the subsequent agency.

FACTS

You retired from California state service on December 30, 2010, as the Acting Secretary of the CTA. You immediately returned to the CTA as a retired annuitant and continued serving as the Acting Secretary until July 5, 2011, when Governor Brown appointed a new Secretary. You stayed on with the CTA to assist with the transition of the new Secretary until the end of September 2011. From April 2008 to November 2010, before your appointment to Acting Secretary in December 2010, you were employed by the CTA as both the Undersecretary and Chief Deputy Director of Technology.

The CTA is responsible for the approval and oversight of information technology projects under Government Code Section 11546. In summary, you state that the CTA is primarily responsible for the state's IT policy, project oversight authority, operational management of the state data center, and advising the Governor on technology issues. The CTA is also responsible for approving the chief information officer that must be appointed by the head of each state agency under Government Code Section 11546.1(a)(1)(c). As the Acting Secretary of the CTA

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 3

your duties included all of the following:

(1) Advising the Governor on the strategic management and direction of the state's
information technology resources.

(2) Establishing and enforcing state information technology strategic plans, policies,
standards, and enterprise architecture.

(3) Minimizing overlap, redundancy, and cost in state operations by promoting the
efficient and effective use of information technology.

(4) Providing technology direction to agency and department chief information officers to
ensure the integration of statewide technology initiatives, compliance with information
technology policies and standards, and the promotion of the alignment and effective management
of information technology services.

(5) Working to improve organizational maturity and capacity in the effective
management of information technology.

(6) Establishing performance management and improvement processes to ensure state
information technology systems and services are efficient and effective.

(7) Approving, suspending, terminating, and reinstating information technology projects.

(8) Performing enterprise information technology functions and service. (See
Government Code Section 11545(b).)

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-
governmental employment provisions known as the permanent and one-year bans. (See Sections
87401 and 87402; Regulations 18741.1 and 18746.1) In addition, Section 87407 prohibits
certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, or using their official
position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment or
have any arrangement concerning employment. (See Regulation 18747.)³ Colloquially, these
provisions are known as the "revolving door" prohibitions.

Permanent Ban

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.)

³ According to the facts you have provided, you have already left the CTA. Accordingly, we are not
providing advice pertaining to Section 87407 at this time.

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 4

The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of absence
from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).) For purposes of the
permanent ban, "[t]he date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment or
takes a leave of absence is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the
duties of the office or employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the
official continues to receive compensation for accrued leave credits." (Regulation
18746.4(a)(1).)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or
any oral or written communication -- or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or
communication -- made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official.

"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application,
request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a
specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . .'" (Section
87400(c).)

An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in
the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . .." (Section 87400(d).)

"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous
proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, licensee, or
approval. (Anderson, supra; Blonier Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.)

As a former officer or employee of the CTA, you were a state employee and are currently
subject to the permanent ban. However, you have not identified any specific judicial or quasi-
judicial proceeding in which you previously participated as an employee or officer of the CTA.
Accordingly, we cannot offer you any additional assistance regarding the permanent ban outside
of the general assistance provided above. If you need additional advice regarding the permanent
ban and any proceeding in which you previously participated as an employee of the CTA, you
should seek additional advice providing the details of the particular proceeding.

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 5


One-Year Ban

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions" or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who held a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. For purposes of the one-year ban, the date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because he or she is on a leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be deemed to have left office permanently if the person merely receives compensation for accrued leave credits. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes

_____________________
⁴ For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action" as the following:

"'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . ." (Section 82020(c))

"'Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section 82037.)

⁵ A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code if the employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on any financial interest. (Section 87302.)

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 6

conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state
agency that the public official worked for or represented; (2) before a state agency "which
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public
official worked for or represented; or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office
during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation
18746.1(b)(6).)

1. Does the one-year ban end on July 5, 2012, 12 months after the new secretary was appointed
or on September 30, 2012, 12 months after you left the CTA?

As the former Acting Secretary, you held a position that is or should be designated in the
CTA's conflict-of-interest code and are therefore subject to the one-year ban for 12 months from
the date you permanently left office or employment with the CTA. As addressed above, the date
on which an official permanently leaves office or employment is the date on which the official is
no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops performing
those duties. (Regulation 18746.4(b).). While a new secretary was appointed July 5, 2011, you
stayed on with the CTA to assist in the transition and were authorized and continued to perform
duties of your office until September 30, 2011. Accordingly, for your purposes, the one-year ban
commenced on September 30, 2011 and will expire September 30, 2012.⁶

2. Is the one-year ban limited to appearances before CTA or is it statewide?

Under the one-year ban appearances and communications are prohibited if they are
before the CTA or before a state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are
subject to the direction and control of the CTA. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).) Based upon the
facts provided, the CTA has oversight of the technology needs for state agencies, and under
Section 11546.1(a)(1)(c) the CTA is responsible for approving the chief information officer that
must be appointed within each state agency. However, the fact that an agency is providing
technical assistance, legal advice, or is subject to oversight by another agency pursuant to state
law, is not a factor in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction and control of
another (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(C)(ii)), and you have not provided any indication that the
CTA has the direction or control over any other agency's budget or day-to-day operations.
Moreover, to the extent that the CTA must approve the appointment of each state agency's chief
information officer by agency heads, this alone does constitute the direction and control of an
agency's personnel. Accordingly, while the one-year ban potentially applies to your appearance
or communications before your former agency, the CTA, and any agency whose budget, personnel,
or operations are subject to the control of the CTA, the one-year ban does not apply to
appearance before other agencies merely because the CTA is responsible for oversight of the
state's technological needs and the final approval of agency appointed chief information officers.

⁶ We also note that you are also subject to the permanent ban as addressed above.

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 7

Notwithstanding the one-year ban, we note that the permanent ban may prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with, or assisting another person other than the State of California, in a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding before any state administrative agency, for compensation, if you previously participated in the proceeding as a state officer or employee.

3. Can you work with other public agencies over which the CTA had no oversight or authority?

As discussed in the answer to question number two, the one-year ban only applies to appearances or communications before the CTA or before a state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of the CTA. However, the permanent ban may apply.

4. Are there any IT procurement restrictions during the one year or permanent ban?

Yes. As addressed above, the one-year ban prohibits you from making any appearance or communication before the CTA, for compensation on behalf of another, for one year after you leave the agency for the purpose of influencing any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." This would include any contracts or negotiations regarding the CTA's IT procurement.

Additionally, the permanent ban prohibits you from ever participating in a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding involving the State of California, or assisting others in the proceeding, for compensation, if you previously participated in the proceeding as an officer or employee of the state. For instance, if you previously participated in a contract between the CTA and your new employer as an employee or officer of the CTA, you are permanently prohibited from participating in any proceeding involving that particular contract on behalf of your new employer if made for the purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the contract.

5. Is there a restriction on appearing before other state departments for which the CTA had project oversight if you were not actively involved in that particular project?

As discussed in the answer to question number two, the one-year ban only applies to appearances or communications before the CTA or before a state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of the CTA. The permanent ban may apply only if you previously participated in the proceeding.

6. Can you work with state departments if you limit your involvement to operational activities with no executive decisions?

Generally, the revolving door provisions apply even if the appearance or communication involves operational activities.

File No. I-12-077
Page No. 8

While we note that the one-year ban does not apply to "[s]ervices performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement" so long as " the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings" (Regulation 18746.1(c)) and that certain ministerial appearances or communications are not for the purpose of influencing under Regulation 18746.2(b), you have not provided any indication that your anticipated appearances or communications before your former agency will be for any reason other than for the purposes of influencing the agency's decisions.

If you need additional assistance determining whether any particular appearance or communication is for "the purpose of influencing" you should seek additional assistance by providing a description of the specific appearance or communication.'

7. What result does working as a retired annuitant have on the one-year and permanent bans?

Should you return to the state as a retired annuitant for a different agency, the one-year and permanent bans would only apply if you make an appearance or communication before your former agency or assist another person in an appearance or communication before the agency, for compensation, in your private capacity. They do not apply to your work performed as a retired annuitant. However, upon leaving the subsequent retired annuitant position, the one-year ban would begin anew.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Keisha O. White
Legal Intern, Legal Division

KOW:jgl

7 Note that communicating with the press, participating as a panelist at a conference or similar public event, and requesting information generally available to the public are not prohibited because these actions are not made for the "purpose of influencing." Similarly, attending informational meetings regarding existing laws, regulations, or policies is not prohibited so long as you do not attempt to influence legislative or administrative actions by your former agency. (Regulation 18746.2(b)(1)-(4).)

sections.question:
As the fonner Acting Secretary of the California Technology Agency (the "CT A"), what 
restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on: 
(1) your future employment in the private sector as an IT procurement agent, who may 
approach government agencies for future sales, or, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assivtance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 831 1-i; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


(2) your ability to work for a different agency as a retired annuitant?
sections.conclusion:
( 1) As the former Acting Secretary of the CT A, the one-year ban prohibits you from 
making an appearance or communication before the CT A, or any state agency whose budget, 
personnel, or operations are under the CT A's direction or control, in representation of another 
person, and for compensation, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative 
action, or any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of 
a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property" such as an 
appearance or communication related to IT procurement, for one year after you leave the agency. 
Additionally, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban that also applies to former state 
employees. Under this ban, you are permanently prohibited from ever participating in a judicial 
or quasi-judicial proceeding involving the State of California, or assisting others in the 
proceeding, for compensation if you previously participated in the proceeding as a state officer 
or employee. 
(2) Also note that should you return to the state as a retired annuitant for a different 
agency, the one-year and permanent bans may potentially apply if in your private capacity and 
for compensation you were to make an appearance or communication or assist another person in 
an appearance or communication before your former agency. Upon leaving the subsequent 
retired annuitant position, the one-year ban would begin anew for appearances before the 
subsequent agency.

Under the Act, public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post­
governmental employment provisions known as the permanent and one-year bans. (See Sections 
87401 and 87402; Regulations 18741.l and 18746.1) In addition, Section 87407 prohibits 
certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, or using their official 
position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment or 
have any arrangement concerning employment. (See Regulation 18747.)3 Colloquially, these 
provisions are known as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
Permanent Ban 
The "pennanent ban" prohibits a fonner st'ate employeeJrom "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or 
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the fonner state employee 
participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.l.) 
3 According to the facts you have provided, you have already left the CTA. Accordingly. we are not 
providing advice pertaining to Section 87407 at this time.  
The permanent ban applies when an official has pennanently left or talces a leave of absence 
from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.l(a)(l).) For purposes of the 
permanent ban. "(t]he date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment or 
talces a leave of absence is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the 
duties of the office or employment. and the official stops perfonning those duties. even if the 
official continues to receive compensation for accrued leave credits." (Regulation 
18746.4(a)(l).) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication -- or aiding. advising. counseling. consulting. or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication -- made with the intent to influence any judicial. quasi-judicial. or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding• means any proceeding. application, 
request for a ruling or other determination. contract, claim, controversy, 
investigation, charge. accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a 
specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . .. " (Section 
87400(c).) 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in 
the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval. disapproval, formal 
written recommendation. rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation. or use of 
confidential information . . .. " (Section 87400(d).) 
"The permanent ban does not apply to a •new' proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
A •new• proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a 
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous 
proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter. No. 1-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not 
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter. No. I-89-649.) A new 
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms. even if it involves the same 
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter. No. 1-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In 
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered 
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or 
approval. (Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) 
As a former officer or employee of the CT A. you were a state employee and are currently 
subject to the peflllanent ban. However, you have not identified any specific judicial or quasi­
judicial proceeding in which you previously participated as an employee or officer of the CTA. 
Accordingly, we cannot offer you any additional assistance regarding the permanent ban outside 
of the general assistance provided above. If you need additional advice regarding the permanent 
ban and any proceeding in which you previously participated as an employee of the CTA, you 
should seek additional advice providing the details of the particular proceeding. 


One-Year Ban  
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, 
any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or 
her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions4 or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 187 46.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who held a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).)5 The ban applies for twelve months from the 
date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. For purposes of the one-year 
ban, the date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment is the date on which 
the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops 
performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because 
he or she is on a leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall 
be deemed to have left office permanently if the person merely receives compensation for 
accrued leave credits. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) While in effect, the one-year ban applies only 
when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(J) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to 'judicial or quasi-judicial" 
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the 
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.l(b)(5).) An 
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal 
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the 
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes

following: 
'"Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,

any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding ... " (Section 82002(a).)

enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the 
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, 
or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action'

conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission,

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented; (2) before a state agency .. which

official worked for or represented; or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and

during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 
18746.l(b)(6).) 
1. Does the one-year ban end on July 5, 2012, 12 months after the new secretary was appointed 
or on September 30, 2012, 12 months after you left the CTA? 
As the fonner Acting Secretary, you held a position that is or should be designated in the 
CTA's conflict-of-interest code and are therefore subject to the one-year ban for 12 months from 
the date you pennanently left office or employment with the CT A. As addressed above, the date 
on which an official pennanently leaves office or employment is the date on which the official is 
no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and stops perfonning 
those duties. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) While a new secretary was appointed July 5, 2011, you 
stayed on with the CT A to assist in the transition and were authorized and continued to perform 
duties of your office until September 30, 2011. Accordingly, for your purposes, the one-year ban 
commenced on September 30, 2011 and will expire September 30, 2012.6 
2. Is the one-year ban limited to appearances before CTA or is it statewide? 
Under the one-year ban appearances and communications are prohibited if they are 
before the CT A or before a state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are 
subject to the direction and control of the CTA. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6).) Based upon the 
facts provided, the CT A has oversight of the technology needs for state agencies, and under 
Section 11546.l(a)(l)(c) the CTA is responsible for approving the chief information officer that 
must be appointed within each state agency. However, the fact that an agency is providing 
technical assistance, legal advice, or is subject to oversight by another agency pursuant to state 
law, is not a factor in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction and control of 
another (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6)(C)(ii)), and you have not provided any indication that the 
CT A has the direction or control over any other agency's budget or day-to-day operations. 
Moreover, to the extent that the CTA must approve the appointment of each state agency's chief 
information officer by agency heads, this alone does constitute the direction and control of an 
agency's personnel. Accordingly, while the one-year ban potentially applies to your appearance 
or communications before your former agency, the CTA, and any agency whose budget, 
personal, or operations are subject to the control of the CTA, the one-year ban does not apply to 
appearance before other agencies merely because the CT A is responsible for oversight of the 
state's technological needs and the final approval of agency appointed chief information officers. 
6 We also note that you are also subject to the pennanent ban as addressed above.  
Notwithstanding the one-year ban. we note that the permanent ban may prohibit you from 
appearing before or communicating with. or assisting another person other than the State of 
California. in a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding before any state administrative agency, for 
compensation. if you previously participated in the proceeding as a state officer or employee. 
3. Can you work with other public agencies over which the CTA had no oversight or authority? 
As discussed in the answer to question number two. the one-year ban only applies to 
appearances or communications before the CT A or before a state agency "which budget. 
personnel. and other operations" are subject to the control of the CT A. However, the permanent 
ban may apply. 
4. Are there any IT procurement restrictions during the one year or pemzanent ban? 
Yes. As addressed above, the one-year ban prohibits you from making any appearance or 
communication before the CT A, for compensation on behalf of another, for one year after you 
leave the agency for the purpose of influencing any discretionary act "involving the issuance, 
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or 
purchase of goods or property." This would include any contracts or negotiations regarding the 
CT A's IT procurement. 
Additionally, the permanent ban prohibits you from ever participating in a judicial or 
quasi-judicial proceeding involving the State of California, or assisting others in the proceeding, 
for compensation, if you previously participated in the proceeding as an officer or employee of 
the state. For instance, if you previously participated in a contract between the CT A and your 
new employer as an employee or officer of the CT A, you are permanently prohibited from 
participating in any proceeding involving that particular contract on behalf of your new employer 
if made for the purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the contract. 
5. ls there a restriction on appearing before other state departments for which the CTA had 
project oversight if you were not actively involved in that panicular project? 
As discussed in the answer to question number two, the one-year ban only applies to 
appearances or communications before the CT A or before a state agency "which budget, 
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of the CT A. The permanent ban may 
apply only if you previously participated in the proceeding. 
6. Can you work witli state departll]ents if you Limit your involvement to operational activities 
with 110 executive decisions? 
Generally, the revolving door provisions apply even if the appearance or communication 
involves operational activities.  
While we note that the one-year ban does not apply to "[s]ervices performed to 
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, 
or sale agreement" so long as " the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, 
or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings" (Regulation 18746.l(c)) and that certain 
ministerial appearances or communications are not for the_purpose of influencing under 
Regulation 18746.2(b), you have not provided any indication that your anticipated appearances 
or communications before your former agency will be for any reason other than for the purposes 
of influencing the agency's decisions. 
If you need additional assistance determining whether any particular appearance or 
communication is for "the purpose of influencing" you should seek additional assistance by 
providing a description of the specific appearance or communication.7 
7. What result does working as a retired annuitant have on the one-year and permanent bans? 
Should you return to the state as· a retired annuitant for a different agency, the one-year 
and permanent bans would only apply if you make an appearance or communication before your 
former agency or assist another person in an appearance or communication before the agency, 
for compensation, in your private capacity. They do not apply to your work performed as a 
retired annuitant. However, upon leaving the subsequent retired annuitant position, the one-year 
ban would begin anew.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As the fonner Acting Secretary of the California Technology Agency (the "CT A"), what 
restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on: 
(1) your future employment in the private sector as an IT procurement agent, who may 
approach government agencies for future sales, or, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assivtance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 831 1-i; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


(2) your ability to work for a different agency as a retired annuitant?

CONCLUSION: ( 1) As the former Acting Secretary of the CT A, the one-year ban prohibits you from 
making an appearance or communication before the CT A, or any state agency whose budget, 
personnel, or operations are under the CT A's direction or control, in representation of another 
person, and for compensation, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative 
action, or any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of 
a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property" such as an 
appearance or communication related to IT procurement, for one year after you leave the agency. 
Additionally, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban that also applies to former state 
employees. Under this ban, you are permanently prohibited from ever participating in a judicial 
or quasi-judicial proceeding involving the State of California, or assisting others in the 
proceeding, for compensation if you previously participated in the proceeding as a state officer 
or employee. 
(2) Also note that should you return to the state as a retired annuitant for a different 
agency, the one-year and permanent bans may potentially apply if in your private capacity and 
for compensation you were to make an appearance or communication or assist another person in 
an appearance or communication before your former agency. Upon leaving the subsequent 
retired annuitant position, the one-year ban would begin anew for appearances before the 
subsequent agency.

(See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment laws that may apply including but not limited to Public Contract Code Sections 10410 and 10411, Government Code Section 1090, or laws regarding incompatible activities such as Government Code Section 19990. QUESTION As the former Acting Secretary of the California Technology Agency (the "CTA"), what restrictions do the Act's revolving door provisions have on: (1) your future employment in the private sector as an IT procurement agent, who may approach government agencies for future sales, or ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-12-077 Page No. 2 (2) your ability to work for a different agency as a retired annuitant? CONCLUSION (1) As the former Acting Secretary of the CTA, the one-year ban prohibits you from making an appearance or communication before the CTA, or any state agency whose budget, personnel, or operations are under the CTA's direction or control, in representation of another person, and for compensation, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property" such as an appearance or communication related to IT procurement, for one year after you leave the agency. Additionally, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban that also applies to former state employees.
